roof_system: Likely low-slope membrane roof with a smooth-coated or aged membrane surface; exact chemistry not discernible from aerial imagery alone

possible_roof_systems.evidence: Large tan/white smooth low-slope fields with relatively uniform appearance and visible rooftop penetrations consistent with a single-ply membrane roof.
Low-slope commercial geometry and muted surface tone could also fit a granulated or weathered membrane field viewed only from overhead.
Flat industrial roof form with limited textural detail could represent a dark membrane, though the visible color here is not typical and confidence is lower.
The tan field appears visually uniform and matte from aerial view, which can be consistent with an aged membrane or coated roof surface.

roof_age_estimate: Likely original or near-original to a 1974 warehouse, though surface treatment/recoat history is unknown; aerial imagery alone cannot confirm age.

notes: Visible localized dark spots and scattered discoloration on the white roof field suggest prior repairs, weathering, or moisture-related staining potential.
Multiple rooftop penetrations/equipment curbs are visible on both roof fields, increasing flashing and leak-maintenance risk.
No clear standing water is visible in this aerial image.
No significant tree overhang or leaf accumulation is visible on the roof surface.

observations: Large low-slope commercial roof with multiple rooftop penetrations, vents, and small equipment curbs.
White roof field shows scattered dark speckling, spot staining, and uneven coloration.
Tan roof field appears more uniform but still has several visible penetrations.
Perimeter drainage details are not clearly readable from this overhead image.
Visible rooftop components increase seal and flashing vulnerability on an older industrial roof.
summary: 03/11/2018 - 03/17/2018 Denver GIS aerial imagery shows an older low-slope industrial warehouse roof in fair overall condition with moderate serviceability risk. The white roof field has scattered dark staining/discoloration and a somewhat uneven appearance that may indicate prior repairs, weathering, or moisture-related concerns, while the tan field appears more uniform. Multiple penetrations, vents, and curbs are visible, increasing flashing and leak potential on a low
recommendation: Have a qualified commercial roofing contractor perform an onsite inspection to verify membrane type, evaluate the visible staining/discoloration, check flashings and penetrations, and perform moisture testing if warranted. If the roof is a restorable low-slope membrane system and no widespread moisture, deck, or substrate failure is found, evaluate a silicone roof restoration option before a more costly overlay or replacement; a qualifying silicone system may reduce tear-off waste, cost, and disruption, create a seamless reflective surface, improve ponding-water tolerance when properly designed and installed, and may qualify for a manufacturer warranty of up to 20 years. If inspection finds widespread moisture, failed insulation, incompatible substrate, or other advanced deterioration, pursue overlay or replacement evaluation instead.
